_meta.authoringNotes: Classification criteria per EULAR/PReS Ankara 2006 and ACR/EULAR 2022 IgA vasculitis criteria. Epidemiology from Gardner-Medwin et al. Lancet 2002 (PMID 11819200) and Calvo-Río et al. Medicine 2016 (PMID 27684849). Nephritis management per KDIGO 2021 and Pillebout & Sunderkötter Semin Nephrol 2018 (PMID 30177008).

h1Title: Allergic Purpura: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Allergic purpura — now formally termed IgA vasculitis (formerly Henoch-Schönlein purpura) — is an immune-mediated small-vessel vasculitis characterized by IgA1-dominant immune deposits in affected tissues, producing a tetrad of palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain, and renal disease. It is the most common systemic vasculitis in children, affecting approximately 10–20 per 100,000 children under 17 years annually in Europe and North America, with peak onset at ages 4–7. Adults develop it less often but follow a more severe course, with a higher risk of persistent kidney disease. Most pediatric cases resolve within 4–6 weeks without treatment, but up to 20–30% of adult patients develop chronic renal impairment. Treatment is supportive in mild disease; corticosteroids and immunosuppressants are reserved for severe abdominal, renal, or systemic involvement.

keyStats.prevalence: 10–20 per 100,000 children per year in Europe (Gardner-Medwin et al. Lancet 2002); 0.1–1.8 per 100,000 adults per year; rising incidence in autumn and winter
keyStats.demographics: Most common in children ages 4–7 years; male-to-female ratio 1.2–2:1 in children; adults have more severe disease with a higher rate of persistent nephritis (up to 30%)
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset age 4–7 in children; in adults typically age 20–60; rare in infants under 2 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 200,000+ cases annually worldwide in children; accounts for up to 50% of childhood systemic vasculitis diagnoses in tertiary pediatric rheumatology series

definition: Allergic purpura (ICD-10: D69.0), also called Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) or IgA vasculitis (IgAV), is a systemic small-vessel vasculitis caused by IgA1-containing immune complex deposition in vessel walls. The IgA1 molecules in affected patients have aberrantly galactose-deficient O-glycans, which are recognized as neoantigens, generating autoantibodies (anti-glycan IgA and IgG). The resulting IgA1-containing immune complexes deposit in the mesangium of the kidney, the small vessels of the skin, the joints, and the bowel wall, activating complement and triggering local inflammation. The condition is classified by the 2022 ACR/EULAR criteria as IgA vasculitis when palpable purpura or petechiae are predominantly in the lower limbs without thrombocytopenia, plus one of: abdominal pain, biopsy showing IgA deposits, arthritis or arthralgia, or renal involvement. IgA vasculitis sits within the spectrum of IgA-mediated diseases alongside IgA nephropathy, and its renal manifestation — IgA vasculitis nephritis — is histologically similar to primary IgA nephropathy. The condition is managed by rheumatologists, hematologists, and nephrologists depending on the dominant organ involved.

typesClassification.type: IgA vasculitis without nephritis (skin-limited or skin-joint-GI)
typesClassification.description: Purpura with or without arthritis and/or abdominal pain, but no urinary abnormalities. The most common presentation in children under 7. Typically self-limiting within 4–6 weeks. No specific treatment beyond NSAIDs for joint pain and analgesia for abdominal cramping.

Renal involvement defined by hematuria (gross or microscopic), proteinuria, or hypertension developing within 6–12 months of purpura onset. Ranges from microscopic hematuria alone (mild) to nephrotic syndrome, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN), and acute kidney injury (severe). Accounts for the majority of long-term morbidity.

typesClassification.type: Severe GI involvement (intussusception, perforation, bowel infarction)
typesClassification.description: Edema and inflammation of the bowel wall from IgA deposits causes severe abdominal pain, hematemesis, hematochezia, and rarely intussusception (1–5% of cases), perforation, or bowel infarction. These are surgical emergencies distinguishable from appendicitis and intussusception from other causes by the concurrent skin and joint involvement.

Adults generally follow a more severe course than children with more significant renal involvement (30–50% develop persistent proteinuria vs 1–5% in children), a higher relapse rate, and a greater likelihood of requiring immunosuppressive therapy. The skin rash may be more extensive and involve the trunk and upper limbs in addition to the lower extremities.

primarySymptoms: Palpable purpura — non-blanching, raised, red-to-purple skin lesions predominantly on the buttocks and lower extremities — the pathognomonic skin finding, present in 100% of cases by definition of current criteria.
Arthritis or arthralgia most commonly affecting the knees and ankles, present in 60–84% of cases; usually non-migratory and non-destructive, resolving completely with the acute episode.
Colicky abdominal pain, often severe, from bowel-wall edema and inflammation; present in 50–80% of cases, may precede the rash by up to 2 weeks and cause diagnostic confusion with appendicitis.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying abdominal pain from bowel inflammation; occasionally with hematemesis or hematochezia from mucosal involvement.

Microscopic or gross hematuria and proteinuria from glomerulonephritis, detectable on urinalysis in 30–50% of patients; usually develops within 4 weeks of rash onset but can appear up to 6 months later.
Low-grade fever (temperature 38–38.5°C) during the acute phase, particularly when preceded by streptococcal upper respiratory tract infection.
Scrotal swelling, pain, and tenderness in boys from testicular vasculitis — present in 2–35% of affected boys; must be distinguished from testicular torsion urgently.
Periarticular soft tissue swelling and tenderness around the ankle and knee without the warm red joint typical of septic arthritis.
Edema of the scalp, periorbital region, dorsal hands and feet, or scrotum from subcutaneous IgA immune complex deposition, more common in younger children.
Hypertension from renal involvement, detected on blood pressure measurement — may be the only sign of significant nephritis.
earlyWarningSigns: Appearance of palpable purpura on the lower extremities and buttocks in a child 1–3 weeks after a respiratory infection — the classic prodrome of IgA vasculitis.

Sudden-onset severe abdominal pain in a child with or without purpura — if purpura appears after an abdominal presentation, it strongly suggests the diagnosis and should prompt IgA vasculitis workup before laparotomy.
New joint swelling and refusal to walk in a child following an upper respiratory tract infection — arthritis preceding or accompanying purpura.
Microscopic hematuria on urine dipstick in a child with skin purpura — the earliest sign of renal involvement requiring close monitoring.
Scrotal swelling or pain in a boy with the classic rash — testicular torsion must be excluded urgently with Doppler ultrasound.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs or rigid abdomen in a known or suspected IgA vasculitis patient — possible intussusception, perforation, or bowel infarction requiring emergency surgery.
Gross hematuria with rising creatinine and hypertension — rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (crescentic nephritis) requiring urgent renal biopsy and immunosuppression.
Nephrotic syndrome: massive proteinuria (> 3.5 g/day), edema, hypoalbuminemia, and hyperlipidemia — urgent nephrology referral.
Scrotal erythema and swelling in a boy with purpura — testicular torsion must be excluded within 6 hours by Doppler ultrasound; torsion causes ischemia if not corrected promptly.
Severe neurological symptoms (seizures, focal deficits, altered consciousness) — rare CNS vasculitis requiring emergency MRI and neurological assessment.

causes.cause: Aberrant IgA1 glycosylation and immune complex formation
causes.description: Patients with IgA vasculitis produce IgA1 molecules with galactose-deficient hinge-region O-glycans, which are recognized as foreign by anti-glycan IgG and IgA autoantibodies. The resulting IgA1-containing immune complexes deposit in small vessel walls, glomerular mesangium, and dermal capillaries, activating complement via the alternative and lectin pathways and recruiting neutrophils, causing local inflammation and vasculitis.

causes.cause: Preceding upper respiratory tract infection (most commonly group A Streptococcus)
causes.description: A preceding upper respiratory tract infection is reported in 30–50% of cases, most often 1–3 weeks before rash onset. Group A Streptococcus is the most commonly identified pathogen; other pathogens include S. aureus, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, parvovirus B19, hepatitis A and B, CMV, and adenovirus. Infection may trigger dysregulation of IgA responses through molecular mimicry or bystander immune activation.

Drugs implicated in triggering IgA vasculitis include ACE inhibitors (captopril, enalapril), antibiotics (penicillin, ampicillin, vancomycin), TNF inhibitors, and vaccines. Drug-induced cases typically follow a benign course that resolves after drug withdrawal, though some cases require corticosteroids.

In adults over 50, IgA vasculitis may be a paraneoplastic manifestation of a solid tumor or lymphoma, which drives aberrant IgA production. A thorough malignancy workup (CT imaging, age-appropriate cancer screening) is recommended for all adults newly diagnosed with IgA vasculitis, as the prevalence of occult malignancy in adult-onset cases is approximately 5–10%.

Several genome-wide association studies have identified HLA associations with IgA vasculitis, overlapping with those for IgA nephropathy. Family clustering occurs in approximately 5% of cases, suggesting a genetic predisposition to dysregulated IgA responses, likely interacting with infectious or environmental triggers.

riskFactors.description: Children in this age group account for the majority of cases; the incidence is roughly 10–20× higher than in adults. The reason is unclear but may relate to the frequency of streptococcal infections and the developmental state of IgA immune regulation in early childhood.

Male-to-female ratio is approximately 1.5–2:1 in pediatric series, though adult disease shows less gender skew. Male sex is associated with higher risk of scrotal involvement and severe abdominal disease in children.
Incidence peaks in autumn and winter, correlating with the higher frequency of group A streptococcal pharyngitis and other respiratory viral infections that trigger IgA vasculitis.

Group A streptococcal pharyngitis in the preceding 1–3 weeks is the most commonly identified trigger. Rapid streptococcal antigen testing and treatment of pharyngitis may reduce but does not eliminate this risk.
IgA nephropathy and IgA vasculitis share the same underlying molecular defect in IgA1 glycosylation; patients with one condition have an elevated risk of developing manifestations of the other.

IgA vasculitis is more common in Caucasians and East Asians than in Black patients, mirroring the ethnic distribution of IgA nephropathy. This pattern suggests a shared genetic susceptibility related to IgA glycosylation pathways.

riskFactors.factor: Drug exposures (ACE inhibitors, antibiotics, TNF inhibitors)
riskFactors.description: Drug-associated IgA vasculitis is more frequent in adults than children. Reviewing and withdrawing potentially causative drugs early may limit disease severity.

affectedDemographics: Children ages 4–7 account for approximately 75% of all cases globally; IgA vasculitis is the most common systemic vasculitis in children.
Male children are affected 1.5–2× more often than female children in most registry data; adult sex distribution is closer to 1:1.
Caucasian and East Asian populations have higher incidence than Black populations — consistent with overlapping genetic risk with IgA nephropathy.
Seasonal variation: 50–60% of pediatric cases in Europe occur in autumn and winter, corresponding to peak streptococcal infection season.
Adults over 50 have a higher frequency of malignancy-associated IgA vasculitis and more severe renal outcomes than younger adults.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis is clinical in most cases. The 2022 ACR/EULAR criteria require palpable purpura or petechiae predominantly in the lower limbs without thrombocytopenia, plus at least one of the following: abdominal pain, biopsy with IgA deposits, arthritis or arthralgia, or renal involvement (proteinuria or hematuria). In the typical child with the full tetrad, no biopsy is needed. Skin or renal biopsy is performed when the diagnosis is uncertain — particularly in adults where other vasculitides (microscopic polyangiitis, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis) enter the differential — or when renal involvement requires histological grading for treatment decisions.

Laboratory workup includes CBC with differential (platelet count normal, distinguishing IgA vasculitis from thrombocytopenic purpura), ESR, CRP, serum IgA level (elevated in 50–60% of patients), serum creatinine and urinalysis with microscopy. Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio quantifies proteinuria. Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) or anti-DNase B titre identifies recent streptococcal infection as a trigger. ANCA testing (anti-MPO and anti-PR3) excludes ANCA-associated vasculitis. In adults, paraneoplastic workup includes CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, as well as age-appropriate cancer screening. Renal biopsy is indicated when proteinuria exceeds 0.5 g/day, creatinine is elevated, or nephrotic syndrome or RPGN is suspected, using the Oxford classification (MEST-C scoring) to guide treatment and prognosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis with microscopy and protein-to-creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hematuria, proteinuria, and casts indicating glomerulonephritis; guides follow-up frequency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clean-catch urine sample analyzed within 2 hours. Repeated weekly for 6 months after diagnosis in all patients, since nephritis can develop weeks after the acute rash. Proteinuria above 0.5 g/day on a spot ratio warrants urgent nephrology referral.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms thrombocytopenia is absent — a critical step to distinguish IgA vasculitis purpura from immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) and meningococcemia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results within 1–2 hours. Platelet count is normal or elevated in IgA vasculitis. Thrombocytopenia redirects the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated in 50–60% of cases; supports diagnosis but is neither sensitive nor specific
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; result within 24–48 hours. A raised IgA in the context of typical clinical features increases diagnostic confidence, but a normal level does not exclude the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) or anti-DNase B titre
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies recent group A streptococcal infection as a trigger; guides antibiotic treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; result within 24–48 hours. ASO rises 1–3 weeks after streptococcal infection and peaks at 3–5 weeks. Positive titre supports streptococcal trigger and justifies a course of penicillin to eradicate carrier state.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin or renal biopsy with immunofluorescence for IgA deposits
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis in atypical presentations or adults; grades renal severity for treatment decisions using Oxford MEST-C classification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin punch biopsy is a minor office procedure under local anesthetic. Renal biopsy is performed under ultrasound guidance (30–45 minutes, conscious sedation); 4–8 hours of observation post-procedure. Immunofluorescence shows dominant IgA deposits in vessel walls (skin) or mesangium (kidney).

diagnosticTests.test: Abdominal ultrasound (in suspected intussusception or severe abdominal pain)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies intussusception, bowel wall edema, or free fluid in children with severe abdominal symptoms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive ultrasound of the abdomen in 15–20 minutes. In suspected ileocolic intussusception, ultrasound shows the characteristic target sign; it can also guide pneumatic or hydrostatic reduction in experienced centers.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment is tailored to disease severity. Mild IgA vasculitis with purpura and joint pain but no significant renal or GI involvement requires only symptomatic management: adequate hydration, rest, paracetamol or NSAIDs (used cautiously in the presence of any renal involvement), and temporary activity restriction to reduce orthostatic purpura exacerbation. Most pediatric cases resolve spontaneously within 4–6 weeks.

NSAIDs and short-course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 1–2 weeks) provide good relief for severe abdominal pain and may reduce the risk of intussusception in small pediatric trials, though they do not prevent nephritis or alter its course once established. The 2012 SHARE consensus and KDIGO 2021 guideline on glomerulonephritis guide management of IgA vasculitis nephritis: mild nephritis (hematuria with proteinuria below 0.5 g/day) is managed with supportive care and monitoring; moderate nephritis (proteinuria 0.5–3 g/day) is treated with ACE inhibitors or ARBs for nephroprotection; severe nephritis (proteinuria above 3 g/day, nephrotic syndrome, RPGN, or crescentic nephritis on biopsy) receives high-dose corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone pulses 500–1,000 mg/day for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with taper over 3–6 months) with or without azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, or cyclophosphamide depending on biopsy findings.

For adults with refractory or relapsing disease, rituximab (anti-CD20) and dapsone have been used with variable evidence. Streptococcal infection, if confirmed, is treated with a 10-day course of penicillin. Colchicine may reduce purpura recurrence in some patients. Regular urinary monitoring for 6–12 months after the acute episode is essential to detect late nephritis.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 5–10 mg/kg 3 times daily, or naproxen) for arthritis and mild abdominal pain
medicalTreatments.description: First-line symptomatic treatment for joint pain and mild abdominal pain. Avoided or used cautiously when renal involvement is present due to risk of further reducing renal blood flow.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid pain relief in 80–90% of patients with arthritis; no evidence that NSAIDs alter the course of nephritis or abdominal involvement

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day, maximum 60 mg, tapering over 2 weeks) for severe GI or systemic involvement
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces bowel-wall edema, relieves severe abdominal pain, and may reduce intussusception risk in some pediatric series. Not proven to prevent nephritis. Also first-line for severe nephritis in adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Severe abdominal pain resolved within 72 hours in 86% vs 49% with placebo (Ronkainen et al. Lancet 2006, PMID 16631911); no effect on nephritis prevention in SHARE/RCT data

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors or ARBs (enalapril 0.1–0.5 mg/kg/day or losartan 0.7–1.4 mg/kg/day) for nephritis with significant proteinuria
medicalTreatments.description: Nephroprotective agents that reduce intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria. First-line for persistent proteinuria above 0.5 g/day in IgAV nephritis, in line with KDIGO 2021 guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce proteinuria by 30–50% in IgA nephropathy and IgAV nephritis per KDIGO 2021; slow CKD progression in patients with proteinuria above 1 g/day

medicalTreatments.name: IV methylprednisolone pulses (500–1,000 mg/day × 3 days) followed by oral prednisolone for severe nephritis or RPGN
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose IV corticosteroids are used for rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, crescentic nephritis on biopsy, or nephrotic-range proteinuria unresponsive to oral therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilizes or improves renal function in 40–65% of IgAV nephritis patients with crescents; response lower in patients with greater than 50% crescents

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine (1–2 mg/kg/day oral) or mycophenolate mofetil (600 mg/m² twice daily) as corticosteroid-sparing immunosuppression
medicalTreatments.description: Added to corticosteroids for moderate-to-severe nephritis requiring prolonged immunosuppression. Azathioprine is preferred in pediatric patients; mycophenolate is increasingly used in adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces proteinuria and permits corticosteroid taper in 50–70% of treated patients per retrospective adult cohort data; RCT evidence is limited

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin V (250–500 mg twice daily for 10 days) for confirmed streptococcal trigger
medicalTreatments.description: Treats the streptococcal infection that triggered the IgA vasculitis episode and eradicates the carrier state. May reduce the duration or severity of the acute episode; does not prevent nephritis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eliminates group A Streptococcus colonization in 90% of patients; association with shorter purpura duration in observational data

surgicalOptions.name: Pneumatic or hydrostatic intussusception reduction
surgicalOptions.description: Under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance, air or fluid is introduced into the colon to push the intussuscepted segment back into normal position. Success rate 70–90% in uncomplicated ileocolic intussusception when bowel viability is maintained.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rate 70–90% for uncomplicated intussusception; recurrence rate 5–10%; surgical reduction required if non-surgical reduction fails or bowel perforation is suspected

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency laparotomy for bowel perforation or failed intussusception reduction
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical resection of infarcted or perforated bowel is required in approximately 0.5–1% of IgA vasculitis cases. Outcomes are favorable when operated early before peritonitis is established.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality below 1% in timely operated cases at pediatric surgical centers; morbidity includes short bowel syndrome if significant intestinal resection is required

alternativeTreatments.name: Dapsone (50–150 mg/day oral) for recurrent purpura
alternativeTreatments.description: An antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory agent that reduces neutrophil infiltration into vessel walls. Used in adults with frequently relapsing purpura; limited evidence from case series and small open-label trials shows reduction in purpura recurrence rates.

alternativeTreatments.name: Colchicine (0.5 mg twice daily oral) for recurrent purpura
alternativeTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory agent used off-label for recurrent IgA vasculitis purpura. A small RCT (Saulsbury J Rheumatol 2009) showed reduction in purpura recurrence; generally well-tolerated with a favorable safety profile.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist or hematologist should evaluate adults with suspected IgA vasculitis, atypical presentations, or evidence of renal involvement, as the differential diagnosis with other vasculitides (ANCA-associated vasculitis, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis) requires expert assessment. A nephrologist is essential when nephritis develops, as biopsy-guided treatment decisions require specialist interpretation.
doctorSelectionGuide: For children, a pediatric rheumatologist at a center with access to renal biopsy, nephrology, and pediatric surgery is ideal. For adults, a center combining rheumatology and nephrology with experience in IgA-mediated disease provides optimal management. Paraneoplastic workup in adults over 50 should involve a generalist physician or oncologist alongside the vasculitis specialist.
hospitalCriteria: Pediatric rheumatology and nephrology specialists with experience in vasculitis
Renal biopsy service with immunofluorescence and Oxford MEST-C scoring capability
Abdominal ultrasound and fluoroscopy for intussusception management
Pediatric surgery on-call for emergency bowel complications
24-hour urine testing and ACR quantification for nephritis monitoring
keyFacilities: Academic medical centers with renal biopsy programs
Pediatric surgery units for GI complications
Centers with IgA nephropathy and vasculitis expertise
preventionStrategies: Promptly diagnose and treat group A streptococcal pharyngitis, the most common identified trigger, to potentially reduce episode severity or duration.
Avoid known drug triggers (ACE inhibitors, ampicillin, vancomycin) in patients with a prior episode of IgA vasculitis where an alternative is clinically feasible.
Ensure up-to-date immunizations per national schedules; avoid live vaccines during active or recent immunosuppressive therapy.
In patients with confirmed IgA vasculitis and nephritis, maintain ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy and regular monitoring to prevent CKD progression.
Patient and family education about the 6–12 month monitoring period for renal involvement, as nephritis can develop weeks to months after the skin episode has resolved.
lifestyleModifications: Rest and elevate the legs during active purpura episodes to reduce gravitational pooling of blood in dependent vessels and limit purpura extension.
Avoid prolonged standing during active disease; return to school or work gradually as purpura resolves.
Monitor urine color and blood pressure at home daily during the acute episode and for 6 months afterward; dark or cloudy urine warrants immediate urinalysis.
Take prescribed ACE inhibitors or ARBs for nephritis consistently and attend scheduled laboratory monitoring.
Avoid NSAIDs when renal involvement is documented or suspected; use paracetamol for pain relief instead.
Avoid contact sports for at least 4 weeks after recovery or until nephritis resolves, as vigorous physical activity may exacerbate proteinuria.

recommended: Adequate fluid intake (1.5–2 L/day in adults) to support renal perfusion and reduce the risk of urinary protein concentration
Low-sodium diet (under 2 g/day) if hypertension or nephrotic syndrome is present to reduce blood pressure and edema
Moderate protein intake (0.8–1 g/kg/day) rather than high protein in the presence of significant proteinuria, to reduce hyperfiltration
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation if prolonged corticosteroid therapy is required, to prevent steroid-induced osteoporosis
avoid: High sodium intake, which exacerbates hypertension and edema in nephritis
NSAIDs and aspirin as self-medication when renal involvement is present
Alcohol, which may worsen systemic inflammation during the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: Activity restriction to bed rest or reduced activity is recommended during active purpura and arthritis, as standing and walking exacerbate gravitational purpura. Light activity can resume within 1–2 weeks of rash resolution. Full activity is permitted once the rash has resolved and urine monitoring is stable. Contact sports should be avoided for at least 4 weeks after nephritis resolves.
dailyManagement: Monitor urine with a dipstick or by visual inspection daily during the acute episode and weekly for 6 months afterward to detect developing nephritis.
Check blood pressure daily in patients with nephritis; hypertension above 130/80 mmHg warrants same-day medical review.
Take prescribed medications (ACE inhibitor, corticosteroid taper) consistently and report side effects promptly.
Keep a symptom diary recording rash distribution, joint pain, abdominal symptoms, urine color, and blood pressure for every follow-up appointment.
Avoid triggers (do not use penicillin-type antibiotics or ACE inhibitors without consulting the treating physician in patients with a prior drug-associated episode).
prognosis: The vast majority of children with IgA vasculitis have an excellent prognosis. Over 90% of pediatric cases resolve within 4–6 weeks, and only 1–5% develop persistent kidney disease beyond 12 months. Renal prognosis is the key long-term determinant: children with isolated microscopic hematuria essentially never develop CKD; those with persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria have a 15–40% risk of renal impairment at 10–20 years. Adults have a significantly worse renal prognosis — 20–30% develop persistent proteinuria and 5–15% reach CKD Stage 3 or above within 10 years. Crescentic nephritis on biopsy (more than 50% of glomeruli with crescents) carries the poorest outlook, with up to 50% reaching end-stage kidney disease within 5 years despite treatment. Recurrences occur in 30–40% of patients, usually within the first year; recurrent episodes are typically milder than the initial episode. Malignancy-associated IgA vasculitis in adults resolves with cancer treatment in most cases.
recoveryTimeline: Purpura resolves in 4–6 weeks in most children; arthritis resolves in parallel. Abdominal pain resolves within days to 2 weeks. Nephritis monitoring continues for 6–12 months after rash resolution. Corticosteroid courses are tapered over 2–6 months for moderate nephritis; immunosuppressants for severe nephritis are maintained 12–24 months.
complications: Chronic kidney disease from persistent IgA vasculitis nephritis — risk is low in children (1–5%) but significant in adults (15–30%); annual urinalysis and BP monitoring for at least 5 years.
Intussusception — occurs in 1–5% of cases, most commonly ileocolic in children; recognized by colicky abdominal pain, bloody stool, and palpable abdominal mass; requires urgent ultrasound.

Orchitis with testicular atrophy — scrotal involvement in boys causes pain and swelling; Doppler ultrasound distinguishes orchitis from testicular torsion; torsion requires emergency surgery within 6 hours.
Bowel perforation or infarction — rare but life-threatening; occurs from ischemic necrosis of the bowel wall in severe GI vasculitis; requires emergency surgery.
Hypertensive emergency from rapidly progressive nephritis — uncontrolled hypertension can cause hypertensive encephalopathy; requires immediate BP lowering.
Corticosteroid-related adverse effects — glucose intolerance, weight gain, growth impairment in children, avascular necrosis, and osteoporosis with prolonged courses.

supportResources.name: Vasculitis UK — Henoch-Schönlein Purpura Information
supportResources.description: Patient information on IgA vasculitis from the leading UK patient charity for vasculitis conditions, including lived experience resources.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — IgA Vasculitis Patient Information
supportResources.description: ACR patient-facing information on diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis of IgA vasculitis for patients and families.

supportResources.name: Kidney Foundation — IgA Nephropathy and IgA Vasculitis
supportResources.description: Educational resources on the renal manifestations of IgA-mediated disease, including monitoring guidance and patient stories.

supportResources.name: KDIGO 2021 Glomerulonephritis Guidelines
supportResources.description: Full KDIGO guideline on the management of IgA vasculitis nephritis, freely accessible for patients, carers, and clinicians.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IgA nephropathy and IgA vasculitis share the same pathological mechanism (IgA1 mesangial deposits) and can be histologically indistinguishable. IgA nephropathy presents with isolated renal disease — hematuria and proteinuria without skin purpura, arthritis, or abdominal pain. IgA vasculitis is diagnosed when systemic features are present alongside renal involvement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SLE can produce a similar purpuric or urticarial skin rash, glomerulonephritis, and arthritis. SLE is distinguished by positive ANA and dsDNA antibodies, complement consumption (low C3/C4), butterfly rash, serositis, and multi-organ involvement not explained by IgA deposits alone. Skin biopsy in SLE shows granular IgG and complement deposits, not IgA-dominant deposits.

confusedWithConditions.name: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ITP causes non-palpable petechiae and purpura from platelet counts below 20,000/µL, without joint, bowel, or renal involvement. Platelet count is normal in IgA vasculitis, and the purpura is typically palpable and lower-limb predominant. A normal platelet count on CBC is the single most important distinguishing test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meningococcemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Meningococcemia causes a rapidly spreading petechial and purpuric rash in the setting of high fever, septic shock, meningismus, and rising inflammatory markers, representing a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate IV ceftriaxone. IgA vasculitis purpura appears over days with normal platelet count and milder systemic symptoms; cultures and blood smear distinguish septicemia.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are immune-complex-mediated systemic diseases with skin, joint, and renal involvement; distinguishing them is essential for correct immunosuppressive treatment.

relatedConditions.name: IgA nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: IgA vasculitis and IgA nephropathy share the same fundamental IgA1 glycosylation defect; renal manifestations are histologically identical.

relatedConditions.name: Acquired hemophilia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are hematological conditions causing abnormal bleeding or purpura; distinguishing them requires coagulation testing and platelet count assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Aplastic anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Aplastic anemia with severe thrombocytopenia can present with purpura mimicking IgA vasculitis; CBC distinguishes pancytopenia from normal counts.

faqs.question: What is allergic purpura?
faqs.answer: Allergic purpura, now called IgA vasculitis (formerly Henoch-Schönlein purpura), is an immune-mediated vasculitis causing inflammation in small blood vessels throughout the body. It produces a distinctive raised purplish rash on the legs and buttocks, joint pain, abdominal pain, and sometimes kidney disease. It is the most common systemic vasculitis in children.

faqs.question: What causes Henoch-Schönlein purpura?
faqs.answer: IgA vasculitis is triggered when IgA antibodies deposit in small blood vessel walls and cause inflammation. A preceding streptococcal sore throat is identified in about 30–50% of cases. Other triggers include viral infections, certain drugs (ACE inhibitors, some antibiotics), and occasionally an underlying malignancy in adults over 50.

faqs.question: Who gets allergic purpura most often?
faqs.answer: Children between ages 4 and 7 are most commonly affected; IgA vasculitis accounts for most systemic vasculitis diagnoses in this age group. Boys are affected slightly more than girls. Adults can develop it too, but they tend to have more severe kidney disease. It occurs more often in autumn and winter following respiratory infections.

faqs.question: What does the rash of Henoch-Schönlein purpura look like?
faqs.answer: The rash is palpable purpura — raised red to purple spots that do not fade when pressed, unlike ordinary blanching rashes. It appears predominantly on the lower legs and buttocks. Spots may start as small reddish bumps and evolve to dark purple patches over hours. New crops of spots can appear for weeks.

faqs.question: Does allergic purpura affect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kidney involvement develops in 30–50% of patients and is the main source of long-term complications. It usually appears as blood and protein in the urine within 4 weeks of the rash. In children, most cases are mild and resolve completely; in adults, 20–30% develop persistent kidney disease requiring long-term monitoring and treatment.

faqs.question: Is Henoch-Schönlein purpura dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most cases in children resolve without long-term consequences. The rare dangerous complications include bowel intussusception or perforation (1–5% of cases), rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis requiring urgent immunosuppression, and in adults, persistent CKD. Adults have a more severe course than children overall.

faqs.question: How is IgA vasculitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: palpable lower-limb purpura without low platelets, plus arthritis, abdominal pain, or renal involvement meets ACR/EULAR 2022 criteria. A normal platelet count distinguishes it from ITP. Elevated serum IgA supports the diagnosis. Skin or renal biopsy confirming IgA deposits is used in atypical cases or to grade kidney severity.

faqs.question: How long does allergic purpura last?
faqs.answer: Most children recover within 4–6 weeks. The rash fades, joint pain resolves, and abdominal pain subsides. About 30–40% of patients have at least one recurrence, usually milder, within the first year. Kidney disease, when present, can persist for months to years and requires follow-up for at least 6–12 months after the rash resolves.

faqs.question: Do children with allergic purpura need steroids?
faqs.answer: Not for mild disease. NSAIDs or paracetamol manage joint pain; most cases resolve without steroids. Oral prednisolone is given for severe abdominal pain, which reduces pain and may prevent intussusception. Steroids are required for significant kidney involvement (nephrotic syndrome or RPGN). Steroids do not prevent nephritis from developing.

faqs.question: Can adults get Henoch-Schönlein purpura?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults develop IgA vasculitis less often than children, but adult cases tend to be more severe. Adults have a higher rate of significant kidney disease (20–30% persistent proteinuria), more frequent relapses, and a greater likelihood of needing immunosuppression. In adults over 50, an underlying malignancy should be excluded.

faqs.question: Can allergic purpura affect the testicles?
faqs.answer: Yes. Boys with IgA vasculitis can develop scrotal swelling and pain from testicular vasculitis in 2–35% of cases. This must be assessed urgently with Doppler ultrasound to exclude testicular torsion, which causes permanent damage within 6 hours. IgA vasculitis orchitis is managed conservatively; torsion requires emergency surgery.

faqs.question: Is Henoch-Schönlein purpura the same as IgA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) was renamed IgA vasculitis (IgAV) by the 2012 Chapel Hill Consensus to reflect the established role of IgA immune complex deposition. The terms refer to the same condition. Allergic purpura is an older name for the same entity, reflecting early observations of its association with allergic-type reactions.

faqs.question: How often should a child be monitored for kidney problems after IgA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: Urine dipstick testing and blood pressure should be checked weekly for the first 4 weeks after diagnosis, then monthly for 6 months. If urinalysis remains normal and BP is controlled after 6 months, most centers discharge from monitoring. Abnormal urinalysis, proteinuria, or hypertension requires nephrology referral and extended monitoring.

faqs.question: What is the difference between IgA vasculitis and IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) affects only the kidneys, producing hematuria and proteinuria without skin rash, joint pain, or abdominal disease. IgA vasculitis has systemic involvement — the purpuric rash is a defining feature. Both share identical renal histology with IgA deposits in the glomerular mesangium and similar treatment for nephritis.

faqs.question: Can allergic purpura be prevented?
faqs.answer: There is no proven prevention. Prompt treatment of streptococcal pharyngitis, the most common trigger, may limit episodes. Avoiding known drug triggers where possible reduces risk of drug-induced cases. There is no vaccine or primary prevention strategy for idiopathic IgA vasculitis.

scrotal swelling in boy with rash
stomach pain and rash in child
metaDescription: Allergic purpura (HSP): IgA vasculitis causing purpura, joint pain, abdominal pain, and kidney disease in children and adults. Symptoms and treatment reviewed.
